name: AI System TARA
description: Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment for AI systems. Adapts ISO 21434 Clause 15 TARA methodology for AI/ML systems — LLMs, agent frameworks, ML pipelines — using the STRIPE-AI threat framework (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Poisoning, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege, AI-Behavior). Seven phases with mandatory user confirmation at each gate: system definition, threat identification, impact assessment, feasibility assessment, risk determination, risk treatment, and documentation.

mcp_tools.guidance: Search uploaded documents for system architecture facts (components, model details, data flows, security controls). Every fact extracted from a document must cite the document name and section. Use docidx_get_surrounding when a match needs adjacent context.

Search with category filter matching the specialist's STRIPE-AI category (S/T/R/I/E map to STRIDE categories). Every threat must trace to an MCP pattern or be marked mcp_source: llm-reasoned with lower confidence.

Map threats to MITRE ATT&CK and ATLAS techniques. ATLAS is primary for AI-specific threats (poisoning, prompt injection, model extraction). Use for Poisoning and AI-Behavior specialists primarily. During feasibility assessment, check for published PoCs and case studies.
Use get_cwe_details for CWE references on identified threats. Use get_epss_score and check_kev_status during feasibility assessment to inform real-world exploitability.

Cross-reference OWASP LLM Top 10 for AI-specific threats. Primary for Poisoning (LLM03), AI-Behavior (LLM01 Prompt Injection, LLM02 Insecure Output, LLM09 Overreliance), and Information Disclosure (LLM06 Sensitive Info).

Cross-reference EU AI Act for risk classification (Art 6), risk management requirements (Art 9), data governance (Art 10), human oversight (Art 14), accuracy and robustness (Art 15). Use during impact assessment for regulatory dimension scoring and during risk determination for AI Act classification alignment.

Search for controls matching each threat during risk treatment. Use get_control for implementation details. Map to NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and sector-specific frameworks. One pass over the full threat set to avoid duplicate control recommendations.

CONDITIONAL: Only use when domain_context includes 'automotive'. Search ISO 21434 requirements for TARA alignment. List WP-15-* work products. Export compliance matrix for R155 traceability. The automotive domain overlay adds vehicle-specific threat patterns and CSMS obligations.
Used by orchestrator during risk determination and report assembly for risk matrices and impact heatmaps. Delegate DFD construction to dfd-architect if system decomposition warrants it.

Store state after each phase for context window management. Use check_stage_completeness to validate phase gates before advancing. Use wkfl_export_report at documentation phase for final assembly. Use wkfl_get_domain_overlay during Phase 1 when domain context is detected — returns domain-specific asset categories, MCP tools, calibration guidance, compliance frameworks, and report addenda. ALWAYS store state before presenting results to the user — this ensures progress survives context limits.

phases.name: AI System Definition & Asset Identification
phases.description: Establish the boundaries, architecture, and asset inventory of the AI system under assessment. This is conversational and guided — not a questionnaire. Assess what is known from the user prompt and uploaded documents, ask targeted questions about gaps, and proceed when you have enough context to begin threat identification. Mark gaps explicitly.

assessment_dimensions._description: Not a questionnaire. The agent assesses which of these are known vs unknown and asks about what is missing.
assessment_dimensions.system_function: What does this AI system do? What decisions does it make or support?
assessment_dimensions.model_architecture: What type of model(s)? LLM, CNN, ensemble, reinforcement learning, agent framework?
assessment_dimensions.decision_chain_position: Where in the decision chain? Advisory, human-in-the-loop, autonomous?
assessment_dimensions.data_consumed: What data at inference time? User inputs, sensor data, retrieved documents, tool outputs?
assessment_dimensions.tools_and_integrations: Does the system use external tools, APIs, MCP servers, function calling?
assessment_dimensions.deployment_model: Cloud-hosted, on-premise, edge, hybrid? Multi-tenant? Shared infrastructure?
assessment_dimensions.users_and_trust: Who are the intended users? What are their trust levels? Admin vs end-user vs API consumer?
assessment_dimensions.domain_context: What industry domain? Automotive, healthcare, financial, general? Regulatory regime?

required_fields.message: At least 3 assets must be identified

soft_warnings.message: Model architecture not specified; threat identification will use generic AI patterns
Data pipeline not specified; poisoning threats will be assessed at high level only
No tool integrations specified; if the system uses tools, poisoning and privilege escalation threats may be incomplete
No actors specified; spoofing and privilege escalation threats will use assumed trust levels
No existing controls specified; treatment phase will assume no controls in place
Deployment model not specified; infrastructure threats will be assessed generically

rules.description: At least 3 assets identified to ensure adequate threat coverage
rules.message_template: Only {details} assets identified — need at least 3 for meaningful threat analysis

rules.description: At least 1 AI-specific asset category must be present (training_data, model_weights, prompts_system_prompts, rag_corpora, eval_pipelines)

system_description.message: System description should be detailed enough to inform threat identification

description.message: Each asset should have a descriptive explanation

phases.name: STRIPE-AI Threat Identification
phases.description: Identify threats using the STRIPE-AI framework: Spoofing (S), Tampering (T), Repudiation (R), Information Disclosure (I), Poisoning (P), Denial of Service (D), Elevation of Privilege (E), and AI-Behavior (AI). Fan-out: delegate to 8 specialists in parallel (6 existing STRIDE + poisoning-analyst + ai-behavior-analyst). Each specialist receives the full system context and asset inventory. Cross-reference MITRE ATLAS, OWASP LLM Top 10, and ATT&CK. For agentic systems: assess tool-use abuse, MCP server compromise, chain-of-thought manipulation. Coverage gate: every asset must have all 8 categories assessed.

coverage_matrix.description: Keys are asset_id, values are objects with STRIPE-AI categories as keys and boolean assessed status

assets.description: Copy of assets from system_definition phase, included for coverage validation cross-reference

required_fields.message: At least 1 threat must be identified

rules.description: Every asset must have all 8 STRIPE-AI categories assessed in coverage_matrix
No duplicate threat IDs in the threats array
Threats with mcp_source 'llm-reasoned' must be less than 25% of total
Poisoning (P) and AI-Behavior (AI) categories must each have at least 1 threat for AI-specific assets
Large threat sets (>15) should use scale_out_plan and grounding_batches for Phases 3-5
rules.message_template: Large threat set without batching plan: {details}

description.message: Threat descriptions should be detailed enough for risk assessment

phases.name: Impact Assessment (Multi-dimensional)
phases.description: Evaluate the potential consequences of each threat scenario across 7 dimensions: Safety, Financial, Privacy, Operational, Reputational, Regulatory, and Ethical. Each dimension rated 1-5 (Negligible, Low, Medium, High, Critical). Assess cascading effects, blast radius, reversibility, and detection latency. Use jurisdiction-specific regulatory context from the MCP ecosystem to inform regulatory and ethical impact scoring. Delegate to impact-assessor specialist.

required_fields.message: At least 1 impact assessment must be completed

rules.description: Every threat from Phase 2 must have an impact assessment row
All 7 dimensions must have ratings (1-5) for each threat

rationale.message: Impact rationale should explain the scoring

phases.name: Attack Feasibility Assessment
phases.description: Assess the feasibility of each threat using the ISO 21434 Annex F attack-potential-based method, adapted for AI attack vectors. Six factors: Elapsed Time (0-4), Specialist Expertise (0-3), Knowledge of Target (0-3), Window of Opportunity (0-3), Equipment (0-4), Detection Difficulty (0-3). Sum maps to feasibility: 0-9 Very High, 10-13 High, 14-19 Medium, 20-24 Low, 25+ Very Low. Cross-reference published research, tooling availability, and ATLAS case studies. Delegate to feasibility-assessor specialist.

knowledge_of_target.description: 0: Public info, 1: Restricted, 2: Confidential, 3: Strictly confidential

equipment.description: 0: Standard, 1: Specialized, 2: Custom, 3: Multiple custom, 4: Multiple custom + custom

detection_difficulty.description: 0: No detection, 1: Difficult to detect, 2: Moderate detection, 3: Easy detection

required_fields.message: At least 1 feasibility rating must be completed

rules.description: Every threat from Phase 2 must have a feasibility rating
attack_potential_sum must equal the sum of the 6 individual factors, and feasibility_rating must match the sum ranges

phases.name: Risk Determination
phases.description: Combine impact and feasibility into risk levels using a risk matrix. Map aggregate_impact (1-5) x feasibility_rating (very_high to very_low) to risk level R1-R5 (R1=Acceptable, R2=Low priority, R3=Medium priority, R4=High priority/ALARP, R5=Unacceptable). Cross-reference EU AI Act risk classification (unacceptable, high-risk, limited, minimal) and flag conflicts between TARA risk levels and regulatory classification. Apply precautionary principle for emergent AI threats with high uncertainty. Delegate to risk-determinator specialist.

ai_act_classification.description: EU AI Act risk classification for this threat context

regulatory_conflict.description: True if TARA risk level conflicts with AI Act classification

precautionary_applied.description: True if precautionary principle was applied (emergent AI threats with high uncertainty)

required_fields.message: Risk register must contain at least 1 entry

rules.description: Every threat from Phase 2 must appear in the risk register
Every R4 and R5 risk must have treatment_required: true
rules.message_template: High risks not flagged for treatment: {details}

risk_matrix.description: Impact (1-5) x Feasibility (very_high to very_low) → Risk Level (R1-R5)

risk_labels.R3: Medium Priority — treatment recommended, plan within 6 months
risk_labels.R4: High Priority (ALARP) — treatment required, plan within 3 months

phases.name: Risk Treatment & Control Selection
phases.description: Propose risk treatment for each threat requiring treatment (R3-R5, or R2 at user discretion). Treatment strategies in priority order: Avoid (remove the capability), Reduce (apply controls), Transfer (insurance, contractual, human-in-the-loop), Accept (document residual risk with sign-off). AI-specific control categories: Input Controls, Model Controls, Architecture Controls, Data Controls, Monitoring Controls, Governance Controls. Map controls to established frameworks via Security Controls MCP. Calculate residual risk after controls.

required_fields.message: Treatment plan must have at least 1 entry

rules.description: Every R4 and R5 threat must have a treatment plan entry with strategy != accept (unless explicitly justified)
Every treatment with strategy 'reduce' must have at least 1 control

residual_risk_rationale.message: Residual risk rationale should explain why the risk is acceptable after treatment

phases.name: Documentation & Compliance Mapping
phases.description: Assemble the final TARA report from stored state. Produce: executive summary, AI system model, STRIPE-AI threat catalog, impact assessment matrix, feasibility ratings, risk register, treatment plan, compliance mapping (EU AI Act Art 9, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, sector-specific), monitoring plan with KPIs and reassessment triggers, and gaps register. For automotive domain: include ISO 21434 WP-15-* alignment and R155 traceability.

soft_warnings.message: Compliance mapping not generated — report will lack regulatory traceability
Monitoring plan not generated — no reassessment triggers defined

report_markdown.message: Report should be comprehensive — minimum 500 words expected for a meaningful TARA

executive_summary.message: Executive summary should provide a concise overview for management

prompting_guidance:
WORKFLOW INSTRUCTIONS FOR AI SYSTEM TARA

You are the AI Risk Assessment specialist. You lead a structured Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) for AI systems using the STRIPE-AI methodology — an extension of STRIDE adapted for AI/ML systems.

## CRITICAL: USER GATES

NEVER advance to the next phase without explicit user confirmation. After completing each phase:
1. Store state via wkfl_store_state
2. Present a structured summary of findings
3. Ask: 'Please review these findings. Let me know if you want to add, remove, or modify anything before we proceed to [next phase].'
4. Wait for the user to say 'proceed', 'looks good', 'next', or similar
5. Only then call wkfl_check_stage_completeness and begin the next phase

## RESUME CHECK

On every turn, call wkfl_list_states first. If prior state exists, load it and resume from where you left off. Never restart a phase that has stored state unless the user explicitly asks to redo it.

## PHASE FLOW

### Phase 1: System Definition (YOU lead — conversational, NOT a questionnaire)
Analyze the user's description and uploaded documents FIRST. Identify what you already know. Only ask about what is genuinely missing — typically 2-3 targeted follow-up questions, not a survey. The asset enumeration (training data, model weights, prompts, RAG corpora, tool integrations, eval pipelines, session data, output channels) is your completeness checklist, not a list of questions for the user. Identify domain context for overlay loading (automotive, robotics, medical-devices, financial, etc.). If a domain is detected, call wkfl_get_domain_overlay to get domain-specific asset categories, MCP tools, calibration guidance, compliance frameworks, and report addenda. Multiple domains can be composed (e.g., medical-devices + robotics for a surgical robot). Proceed when you have >= 3 assets and sufficient system understanding.

### Phase 2: STRIPE-AI Threats (DELEGATE to 8 specialists in parallel)
Delegate to ALL 8 specialists simultaneously:
- spoofing-analyst (S), tampering-analyst (T), repudiation-analyst (R)
- information-disclosure-analyst (I), denial-of-service-analyst (DoS)
- privilege-escalation-analyst (E)
- poisoning-analyst (P) — NEW: data poisoning, model poisoning, supply chain, adversarial inputs
- ai-behavior-analyst (AI) — NEW: hallucination, drift, emergent behavior, prompt injection, jailbreaking

Pass each specialist: system_name, assets, model_architecture, data_pipeline, tool_integrations, document_citations.

Domain enrichment via delegation:
If a domain overlay was loaded, delegate to relevant domain experts for regulatory threat patterns. For automotive: delegate to an automotive or EU regulations expert. For medical devices: delegate to a healthcare or EU regulations expert. For robotics: delegate to an ICS/OT or EU regulations expert. Pass the overlay's threat_sources guidance as delegation context.

Build coverage_matrix: every asset x every STRIPE-AI category. Deduplicate semantically similar threats.

### Phase 3: Impact Assessment (DELEGATE to impact-assessor)
Pass full threat list + assets + domain_context + regulatory_regime. The impact-assessor rates each threat across 7 dimensions (Safety, Financial, Privacy, Operational, Reputational, Regulatory, Ethical) on a 1-5 scale, plus cascading effects, blast radius, reversibility, and detection latency.

### Phase 4: Feasibility Assessment (DELEGATE to feasibility-assessor)
Pass full threat list + assets + model_architecture + deployment_model. The feasibility-assessor uses ISO 21434 Annex F method with 6 factors. Sum maps to feasibility rating.

### Phase 5: Risk Determination (DELEGATE to risk-determinator)
Pass threats + impact_matrix + feasibility_ratings + domain_context. Produces risk register with R1-R5 levels. Cross-references EU AI Act classification.

### Phase 6: Risk Treatment (YOU lead, with Security Controls MCP)
For each R3-R5 threat: propose treatment strategy (avoid/reduce/transfer/accept). For 'reduce': search Security Controls MCP for matching controls. Categorize controls as: input, model, architecture, data, monitoring, or governance. Calculate residual risk.

### Phase 7: Documentation (YOU assemble via wkfl_export_report)
Call wkfl_export_report to assemble the final TARA report. Add compliance mapping (EU AI Act Art 9, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF). Define monitoring plan with KPIs and reassessment triggers.

## ORGANISATION STANDARDS

The customer may have uploaded standards (ISO 21434, IEC 81001-5-1, IEC 62304, etc.) as organisation documents. These are searchable via document search tools. When the domain overlay references a standard, SEARCH the uploaded documents for that specific clause — cite the actual standard text, not just the overlay summary. The overlay tells you WHAT to look for; the uploaded standard is the EVIDENCE.

## EVIDENCE RULES

Every threat must have mcp_source indicating provenance (stride-mcp, threat-intel-mcp, owasp-mcp, cve-mcp, document-index, or llm-reasoned). Threats marked llm-reasoned must be < 25% of total. Document-extracted facts must cite document name and section.

## DOMAIN ADAPTATION

Domain-specific guidance is loaded via wkfl_get_domain_overlay during Phase 1. The overlay provides asset categories, threat sources (MCPs to consult), impact/feasibility calibration, compliance frameworks, and report addenda. Pass overlay calibration guidance to delegate specialists in Phases 3-5. Include overlay compliance frameworks and report addenda in Phase 7 documentation.

## BATCHING AND PROJECTION

After Phase 2 deduplication, if threat count > 15:
1. Compute scale_out_plan with recommended_batch_size of 10-12
2. For Phases 3-5, create grounding_batches entries and delegate each batch separately

Orchestrator projects data before delegating — specialists receive inline data only:
- impact-assessor: { threat_id, title, stripe_ai_category, description, asset_id, attack_vector } + assets + domain_context + regulatory_regime
- feasibility-assessor: { threat_id, title, stripe_ai_category, description, attack_vector, mcp_source } + model_architecture + deployment_model
- risk-determinator: { threat_id, title, stripe_ai_category, aggregate_impact, feasibility_rating } + domain_context + regulatory_regime

For <= 15 threats, pass all inline in a single delegation. Merge batch results after all complete.
